sanskrit_devanagari:
कायेन मनसा बुद्ध्या केवलैरिन्द्रियैरपि ।
योगिनः कर्म कुर्वन्ति सङ्गं त्यक्त्वात्मशुद्धये ॥ ११ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
kāyena manasā buddhyā kevalair indriyair api |
yoginaḥ karma kurvanti saṅgaṁ tyaktvātma-śuddhaye || 11 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical; vedabase renders without explicit pāda breaks but body text matches).
exact (transliteration body identical to vedabase; punctuation and pāda-break rendering differ between sources).

translations.verbatim_quote: The yogīs, abandoning attachment, act with body, mind, intelligence and even with the senses, only for the purpose of purification.
The yogis, abandoning attachment, act with body, mind, intelligence and even with the senses, only for the purpose of purification.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 1430 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: When one acts in Kṛṣṇa consciousness for the satisfaction of the senses of Kṛṣṇa, any action, whether of the body, mind, intelligence or even the senses, is purified of material contamination. There are no material reactions resulting from the activities of a Kṛṣṇa conscious person.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 5.11 Since tyaktva, by giving up sangam, attachment with regard to results; yoginah, the yogis, men of action; kurvanti, undertake; karma, work; kevalaih, merely- this word is to be construed with each of the words, body etc., so as to deny the idea of ownership with regard to all actions-; kayena,…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-5-11-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara: the yogis (karma-yogins, here distinct from the established jñānin of 5.8-5.9) act with body, mind, intellect, and even with the senses alone — having abandoned attachment, for the purification of the self (antaḥkaraṇa-śuddhi). The verse names the karma-yogin's full-faculty engagement: every layer of the apparatus is engaged, none withheld.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 5.11 Renouncing attachment to heaven etc., the Yogins perform actions accomplishable by the body, the mind and the intellect for the purification of themselves, i.e., for annulling the bonds of his previous Karma which have afected the self and which involve the self in Samsara.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-5-11-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja: karma-yogins perform action with body, mind, intellect, and the senses, having abandoned the attachment that consists in identifying these instruments with the self. The action is for the purification of the self — meaning, for the loosening of the karma-binding that obscures self-knowledge.

positions.rendering: antaḥkaraṇa-śuddhi (purification of the inner instrument as preparation for jñāna)
loosening of karma-binding that obscures self-knowledge

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All three preserve self-purification as karma-yoga's purpose. Shankara reads it as preparation for jñāna; Ramanuja reads it as karma-loosening preparatory to self-realization; Prabhupada renders devotionally.

literal_meaning: Yogis perform action with the body, the mind, the intellect, and even with mere senses, having abandoned attachment, for the purification of the self.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna names what the karma-yogin engages: kāya (body), manas (mind), buddhi (intellect), and even kevalaiḥ indriyaiḥ (the senses alone). All faculties active. The sole withholding is saṅga (attachment). The purpose is ātma-śuddhi (self-purification). The verse rejects partial engagement as a misreading of karma-yoga: every layer is in the work; only attachment is not.
